flashcards.front: What are the three levels of the CUDA thread hierarchy?
flashcards.back: Threads (smallest unit), Blocks (groups of threads that share an SM and shared memory), and Grids (collection of all blocks launched by a single kernel). Grids and blocks can be 1D, 2D, or 3D.

flashcards.front: What are the four key CUDA built-in variables for thread indexing?
flashcards.back: threadIdx (thread index within block), blockIdx (block index within grid), blockDim (threads per block), and gridDim (blocks in the grid). All are dim3 types with .x, .y, .z components.

flashcards.front: What is the global thread index formula for 1D?
flashcards.back: globalIdx = blockIdx.x * blockDim.x + threadIdx.x. This gives each thread a unique index across the entire grid.

flashcards.front: What is occupancy and what limits it?
flashcards.back: Occupancy = active warps / max warps per SM. Limited by: registers per thread (fewer threads if more registers), shared memory per block (fewer blocks if more shared memory), and block size (must form complete warps of 32).

flashcards.front: Why must __syncthreads() be called by ALL threads in a block?
flashcards.back: It is a barrier synchronization -- all threads must reach the point before any can proceed. Placing it inside a conditional that only some threads enter causes undefined behavior (potential deadlock) because waiting threads will never be released.
